lessons.title: What is AI really - and what is it NOT for?
lessons.content:
# Lesson 1: What is AI really - and what is it NOT for?

**In one sentence:** Let's start with the clear: what you can ask AI and what you shouldn't entrust it to.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** AI usage map for 3 tasks

## Learning goal

Speeding up daily customer service tasks with data protection risk. In this situation, you are a customer service coordinator and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
By the end of today's practical task, you will have an AI usage map for 3 tasks, which you can use again tomorrow.
Key insight: Clarifying the role of AI starts to work when you make the output measurable right from the start.

At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **separate the tasks that are suitable for AI and those that are not**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **AI usage map for 3 tasks**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **Correct risk classification for 3/3 tasks**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step to handle the main risk: **accidental entry of sensitive data**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** AI usage map for 3 tasks
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-rutin/day-01-what-is-ai-actually-and-what-is-not-valo.md`

## Who

**Primary Role:** Customer Service Coordinator
**Secondary role:** data protection officer
**Involved decision-maker:** executive

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows you in practice how to apply the **Clarifying the role of AI** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- AI is a tool, not a decision maker. A good result requires a clear goal and human control.
- The key is that you always translate the task into a clear output.
- Quality comes from ensuring that the AI ​​usage map can be checked and repeated for 3 tasks.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- acceleration of daily customer service tasks in situations with a data protection risk, when a quick and clear decision is required
- when an AI usage map gives a clear next step to several people for 3 tasks

### Do not use this when

- if the risk of accidental entry of sensitive data cannot be covered by human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between the customer service coordinator and the data protection officer
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where a correct risk classification counts for 3/3 tasks

## When

### Use it when

- speeding up daily customer service tasks, you face a task with a data protection risk, and time is short
- AI usage map based on 3 tasks, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- AI usage map needs to be rewritten every time for 3 tasks
- you often get back that the content does not handle the problem of accidentally entering sensitive data

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- an AI usage map for 3 tasks is created faster, with fewer rounds
- quality will be more measurable with a correct risk classification indicator for 3/3 tasks
- rework is reduced, because you filter out the error of accidentally entering sensitive data early

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases in a situation where daily customer service tasks are accelerated in addition to data protection risks
- it is more difficult to maintain the correct risk classification target for 3/3 tasks

### Realistic expectation
- It will improve: clarifying the role of AI, its practical application and the quality of the transferable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying particular attention to **accidental entry of sensitive data** risk. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the format of the AI usage map for 3 tasks is clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether the correct risk classification measurement point has been met for 3/3 tasks.


**Don't**
- Do not leave the topic of accidentally entering sensitive data in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Do not ask for a general text if you need material for a specific decision for the task of speeding up daily customer service tasks with data protection risk.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** too general a request to speed up daily customer service tasks with a data protection risk. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and the correct risk classification measurement for 3/3 tasks. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] **AI usage map for 3 tasks** output is ready and saved.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the **Correct risk classification** indicator for task 3/3.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a brief description of a real task from the following situation: **Acceleration of daily customer service tasks with data protection risk**
- a short sample of your own, which shows what you consider the output of clarifying the role of good AI
- checklist to exclude the risk of accidental entry of sensitive data

### Steps

1. Write in 4 lines the situation, the goal and how you will measure the correct risk classification result for 3/3 tasks.
2. Create a request sample for generating an AI usage map for 3 tasks, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Speeding up daily customer service tasks with data protection risk | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | AI usage map for 3 tasks | Saved, reusable template |

> **Pro tip:** AI usage map for 3 tasks will be stable if you check it with the same 3 questions after each run.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose one of your own tasks for tomorrow where the same type of decision is required as in the case of speeding up daily customer service tasks with a data protection risk. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: correct risk classification for 3/3 tasks.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the AI ​​usage map 3 tasks more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] AI usage map for 3 tasks completed and can be shared.
- [ ] For 3/3 tasks, a correct risk classification measurement point is recorded.
- [ ] you ran a separate preventive step for the risk of accidentally entering sensitive data.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** Correct risk classification for 3/3 tasks
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **OpenAI Documentation - Guidelines**. OpenAI. 2026. Read: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides
2. **NIST AI Risk Management Framework**. NIST. 2023. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework

## Further reading

1. **OpenAI - Request Writing Guide** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering
2. **Nielsen Norman Group - Surface Copywriting** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/

# Lesson 2: The 4 elements of a good request **In a sentence:** A good request is shorter than you think, but much more precise.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** request card: purpose, context, form, constraint

## Learning goal

Writing an email to a key customer about a deadline slip. In this situation, you are a junior marketer and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
At the end of today's practical task, you will have a request card in your hand: goal, context, form, limit, which you can use again tomorrow.
Key insight: The 4-element request structure starts working when you make the output measurable at the start. At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **transform basic tasks into structured requests**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **request card: goal, context, form, limit**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **every request contains the 4 mandatory elements**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step to handle the main risk: **generic and unusable output**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** request card: purpose, context, form, limit
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-rutin/nap-02-a-jo-keres-4-eleme.md`

## Who

**Primary role:** junior marketer
**Secondary role:** sales manager
**Involved decision maker:** key customer

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows in practice how to apply the **4 element request structure** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- The model fulfills what you specifically ask for. Format and limit increase accuracy.
- Fewer but more precise instructions typically give better results.
- Quality comes from ensuring that the request card: purpose, context, form, limit is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- writing an email about a deadline slip to a key customer in situations where a quick and clear decision is needed
- when a request card: goal, context, form, limit gives a clear next step to several people

### Do not use this when

- if you cannot cover the risk of general and unusable output with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between junior marketing and sales manager
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where each request contains the 4 mandatory elements

## When

### Use it when

- you encounter the task of writing an email about a deadline slip to a key customer, and you have little time
- request card: based on purpose, context, form, limitation, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- request card: goal, context, form, limit need to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content doesn't handle generic and useless output problem

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- a request card is created faster: goal, context, form, limit, with fewer rounds
- the quality will be more measurable, all requests include the 4 mandatory elements with indicators
- the rework is reduced, because you filter out general and unusable output errors early

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases in the situation of writing an email to a key customer about a deadline slip
- it is more difficult to keep every request contains the 4 mandatory elements goal

### Realistic expectations

- It will improve: the practical application of the 4-element request structure and the quality of the deliverable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying special attention to the risk of **general and unusable output**. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the format of the request card: goal, context, form, limit is clear from the beginning.
- Before finalizing, check whether all requests include the 4 mandatory elements measuring points.


**Don't**
- Don't leave a generic and useless output topic in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Do not ask for a general text, if a specific decision requires writing an email to a key client about a material deadline slip.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** an overly general request to write an email about a deadline slip to a key customer is initiated. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and each request contains the 4 mandatory elements between measurements. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] **request card: goal, context, form, limit** output is ready and saved.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the indicator **every request contains the 4 mandatory elements**.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a short description of a real task about the following situation: **Writing an email to a key customer about a deadline slip**
- a short own sample that shows what you consider a good 4-element request structure output
- checklist to rule out the risk of general and unusable output

### Steps

1. Write down the situation, the goal and how you will measure it in 4 lines, each request contains the 4 mandatory elements results.
2. Create a request sample to create a request card: purpose, context, form, limit, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Writing an email about a deadline slip to a key customer | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | request card: purpose, context, form, limit | Saved, reusable template |

> **Common mistake:** Many people only look at the risk of generic and unusable output at the end, even though it should be indicated in the task description.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose your own task for tomorrow, which requires the same type of decision as in the case of writing an email to a key client about a deadline slip. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: every request contains the 4 mandatory elements.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the request card: purpose, context, form, limit more precise.

## Self-check

- [ ] request card: purpose, context, form, limit completed and shareable.
- [ ] all requests include the 4 mandatory elements measurement points are fixed.
- [ ] you ran a separate preventive step for general and unusable output risk.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** all requests contain the 4 mandatory elements
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **NIST AI Risk Management Framework**. NIST. 2023. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework
2. **Nielsen Norman Group - UX copywriting**. NN/g. 2024. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/

## Further reading

1. **Nielsen Norman Group - Surface Copywriting** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/
2. **NIST - AI Risk Management Framework** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework

# Lesson 4: Style and voice - teach him to "write like you" **In a sentence:** The tone of voice holds the brand image together, so this must also be taught to the AI.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** personal style brief and blacklist

## Learning goal

Same message to manager and customer in different tone. In this situation, you are a freelance copywriter and you need to make a quick but reliable decision.
At the end of today's practical task, you will have a personal style task description and prohibition list, which you can use again tomorrow.
Key insight: setting the style and sound starts working when you make the output measurable at the start. At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **create texts adjusted to your own tone**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **personal style task description and ban list**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **2 versions with suitable tone for 2 target groups**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step to deal with the main risk: **communication not suitable for the target audience**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** personal style task description and ban list
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-routine/day-04-stylus-and-sound-teach-me-to-write-like-you.md`

## Who

**Primary role:** Freelance copywriter
**Secondary Role:** Brand Manager
**Involved decision maker:** customer relationship manager

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows in practice how to apply the **style and voice adjustment** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- The style can be reproduced if you provide a sample, rule and counterexample.
- In real work, a quick fix is ​​more important than a perfect first round.
- The quality here is provided by the fact that the personal style task description and ban list is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- the same message to the manager and the customer in a different tone of voice in situations where a quick and clear decision is required
- when a personal style task description and blacklist give several people a clear next step

### Do not use this when

- if communication is not suitable for the target audience, you cannot cover the risk with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between freelance copywriter and brand manager
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where 2 versions with the appropriate tone count for 2 target groups

## When

### Use it when

- the same message to the manager and the customer, in a different tone, you encounter a task, and time is short
- based on the personal style task description and ban list, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- personal style task description and prohibition list need to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content does not deal with the problem of communication that is not suitable for the target audience

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- a personal style task description and ban list are prepared faster, with fewer rounds
- the quality will be more measurable for 2 target groups with indicators of 2 versions with appropriate tone
- rework is reduced, because you filter out communication errors that are not suitable for the target audience early on

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases when the same message is sent to the manager and the customer in a different tone
- it is more difficult to keep 2 targets for 2 target groups with a suitable tone of voice

### Realistic expectations

- It will improve: the practical application of style and tone settings and the quality of the transferable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying particular attention to the risk of **communication not suitable for the target audience**. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that your personal style task description and blacklist format are clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether 2 measurement points for 2 versions with appropriate tone have been met for 2 target groups.


**Don't**
- Do not leave the topic of communication that does not suit the target audience in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Don't ask for a general text if you need a specific decision, the same message for the manager and the client in a different tone for the task.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** too general a request is sent for the same message to the manager and customer in a different tone for a situation. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and the measurement of 2 versions with the appropriate tone for 2 target groups. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] The **personal style task description and ban list** output has been completed and saved.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented pointers to **2 versions with appropriate tone for 2 target groups**.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a short description of a real task about the following situation: **Same message to manager and customer in different tone**
- a short own sample that shows what you consider a good style and sound setting as an output
- checklist to exclude the risk of communication not suitable for the target audience

### Steps

1. In 4 lines, write down the situation, the goal and how you will measure the results of 2 versions with a suitable tone for 2 target groups.
2. Prepare a request sample for the production of a personal style task description and blacklist, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Same message to manager and customer in different tone | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | personal style task description and ban list | Saved, reusable template |

> **Common mistake:** Many people only look at the risk of communication that is not suitable for the target audience at the end, even though it should already be indicated in the task description.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose a task of your own tomorrow, where the same type of decision is required, as in the case of the same message to the manager and the customer in a different tone. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: 2 versions with the right tone for 2 target groups.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make your personal style brief and blacklist more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] personal style task description and ban list completed and can be shared.
- [ ] For 2 target groups, 2 measurement points of versions with appropriate tone are recorded.
- [ ] you ran a separate preventive step for the risk of communication not suitable for the target audience.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** 2 versions with appropriate tone for 2 target groups
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **Atlassian Teamwork Playbook**. Atlassian. 2025. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook
2. **OpenAI documentation - Guidelines**. OpenAI. 2026. Read: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides

## Further reading

1. **Atlassian - Teamwork Playbook** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook
2. **OpenAI - Request Writing Guide** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering

lessons.title: Email in minutes - in a professional voice
lessons.content:
# Lesson 6: Email in minutes - in a professional voice

**In one sentence:** A professional email can be accurate and humane even in 3 minutes.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** Email template that can be used for 3 situations

## Learning goal

Complaint handling, status reporting and follow-up in one day. In this situation, you are a customer relationship manager and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
By the end of today's practical task, you will have an email template that can be used for 3 situations, which you can use again tomorrow.
Key insight: writing business emails starts to work when you make the output measurable right from the start.

At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **write consistent responses to different email situations**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **email template that can be used for 3 situations**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **all three emails contain a clear CTA**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step to handle the main risk: **too long or uncertain message**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** Email template that can be used for 3 situations
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-rutin/nap-06-email-minutes-alatt-profi-hangon.md`

## Who

**Primary Role:** Customer Relationship Manager
**Secondary Role:** Sales Support Associate
**Involved decision-maker:** partnership manager

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows in practice how to apply the **business email writing** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- The purpose of the email is to elicit a decision or response. Brevity + a clear request gives results.
- Fewer but more precise instructions typically give better results.
- Quality comes from ensuring that the email template can be used for 3 situations and is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- complaint handling, status reporting and follow-up in one-day situations when a quick and clear decision is needed
- when an email template that can be used for 3 situations gives several people a clear next step

### Do not use this when

- if the message is too long or uncertain, you cannot cover the risk with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between customer relations manager and sales support staff
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks where a clear CTA is included in all three emails counts

## When

### Use it when

- complaint handling, status reporting and follow-up, one day you will encounter a task, and time is short
- Based on an email template that can be used for 3 situations, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- Email template that can be used for 3 situations needs to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content does not handle the problem of too long or uncertain messages

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- email templates for 3 situations can be created faster, with fewer rounds
- the quality will be more measurable, all three emails have a clear CTA with an indicator
- rework is reduced, because you filter out too long or uncertain message errors early on

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings in complaint handling, status reporting and follow-up in a one-day situation increases
- it is more difficult to keep a clear CTA goal in all three emails

### Realistic expectations

- It will improve: the practical application of business email writing and the quality of the deliverable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying special attention to **too long or uncertain message** risk. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the format of the email template that can be used for 3 situations is clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether all three emails have a clear CTA measurement point.


**Don't**
- Do not leave too long or uncertain message topics in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Don't ask for a general text if you need material for a specific decision, complaint handling, status report and follow-up for a task in one day.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** too general a request for complaint handling, status report and follow-up is started for a situation in one day. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and a clear CTA in all three emails. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] You have completed and saved **email template** output that can be used for 3 situations.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the **clear CTA in all three emails** indicator.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a brief description of a real task about the following situation: **Complaint handling, status report and follow-up in one day**
- a short personal sample that shows what you consider a good business email output
- checklist to exclude the risk of too long or uncertain messages

### Steps

1. In 4 lines, write down the situation, the goal and how you will measure the results in all three emails with a clear CTA.
2. Create a sample request to create an email template that can be used for 3 situations, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Complaint handling, status reporting and follow-up in one day | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | Email template for 3 situations | Saved, reusable template |

> **Common mistake:** Many people only look at the risk of a too long or uncertain message at the end, even though it should already be indicated in the task description.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose your own task for tomorrow, which requires the same type of decision as complaint handling, status reporting and follow-up in the case of one day. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: all three emails contain a clear CTA.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the email template for 3 situations more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] An email template that can be used for 3 situations is ready and can be shared.
- [ ] a clear CTA is included in all three emails, a measurement point is recorded.
- [ ] You ran a separate preventive step for the risk of too long or uncertain messages.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** all three emails contain a clear CTA
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **NIST AI Risk Management Framework**. NIST. 2023. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework
2. **Nielsen Norman Group - UX copywriting**. NN/g. 2024. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/

## Further reading

1. **Nielsen Norman Group - Surface Copywriting** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/
2. **NIST - AI Risk Management Framework** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework

**Don't**
- Don't leave the topic of losing the point in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Don't ask for a general text if you need material for a specific decision, hand over 20 pages of background material to the manager and the team for the task.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** too general a request to hand over 20 pages of background material to the manager and team for a situation. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and 5 key information measurements in all three versions. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] **3-level document package** output is ready and saved.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the indicator **5 key information is included in all three versions**.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a short description of a real task about the following situation: **Handing over 20 pages of background material to the manager and team**
- a short sample of your own, which shows what counts as output for you in creating a good task description, outline, summary
- checklist to exclude the risk of losing the essence

### Steps

1. Write down the situation, the goal and how you will measure the results in 4 lines. 5 key pieces of information are included in all three versions.
2. Prepare a sample request for the production of a 3-level document package, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Handing over 20 pages of background material to the manager and team | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | 3 Level Document Package | Saved, reusable template |

> **Common mistake:** Many people only look at the risk of missing the point at the end, even though it should already be indicated in the task description.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: 5 key pieces of information are included in all three versions.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the 3-level document package more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] A 3-level document package is ready and can be shared.
- [ ] 5 key information is included in all three versions, measurement point is fixed.
- [ ] you ran a separate preventive step for the risk of losing the essence.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** 5 key pieces of information are included in all three versions
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

1. **Atlassian Teamwork Playbook**. Atlassian. 2025. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook

# Lesson 9: Spreadsheet Thinking with AI

**In one sentence:** The table is not a formal trick, but a decision support tool.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** comparative decision table

## Learning goal

Comparison of 3 supplier offers. In this situation, you are a procurement employee and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
By the end of today's practical task, you will have a comparative decision board.
Key insight: tabular thinking starts to work when you make the output measurable right from the start.

At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **convert text input into a comparable table**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **comparative decision table**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **at least 4 common evaluation criteria**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step for handling the main risk: **mixing of aspects**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** comparative decision table
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-rutin/nap-09-tablazat-gondkolkodas-ai-val.md`

## Who

**Primary Role:** Purchasing Associate
**Secondary role:** financial analyst
**Involved decision-maker:** purchasing manager

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows in practice how to apply the **thinking table** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- The structure reduces distortion. There is no fair comparison without uniform criteria.
- The key is that you always translate the task into a clear output.
- The quality here is provided by the fact that the comparative decision table is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- Comparison of 3 supplier offers in situations where a quick and clear decision is required
- when a comparative decision board gives several people a clear next step

### Do not use this when

- if you cannot cover the risk of mixing aspects with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between procurement staff and financial analyst
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where at least 4 common evaluation criteria matter

## When

### Use it when

- You are faced with the task of comparing 3 supplier offers, and time is short
- based on a comparative decision table, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- comparative decision table needs to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content does not handle the problem of mixing aspects

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- a comparative decision board is prepared faster, with fewer rounds
- quality will be more measurable with at least 4 common evaluation criteria indicators
- the rework is reduced, because you filter out errors of mixing aspects early

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases in the situation of comparing 3 supplier offers
- it is more difficult to maintain at least 4 common evaluation criteria goals

### Realistic expectation
- It will improve: the practical application of tabular thinking and the quality of the transferable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying special attention to the risk of **mixing of points of view**. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the format of the comparative decision table is clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether at least 4 common evaluation criterion measurement points have been met.


**Don't**
- Don't leave the topic of mixing aspects in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Don't ask for a general text if you need material for a specific decision for the task of comparing 3 supplier offers.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** an overly general request is made for the situation of comparing 3 supplier offers. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and at least 4 common evaluation criteria measurements. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] You have completed and saved **comparative decision table** output.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the **at least 4 common evaluation criteria** indicators.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a short description of a real task about the following situation: **Comparison of 3 supplier offers**
- a short sample of your own, which shows what you consider a good tabular thinking output
- mixing of checklist aspects to exclude risk

### Steps

1. In 4 lines, write down the situation, the goal and how you will measure at least 4 common evaluation criteria results.
2. Prepare a sample request for the production of a comparative decision table, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Comparison of 3 supplier offers | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | comparative decision table | Saved, reusable template |

> **Pro tip:** a comparative decision table will be stable if you check it with the same 3 questions after each run.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose your own task for tomorrow, where you need to make the same type of decision as in the case of comparing 3 supplier offers. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: at least 4 common evaluation criteria.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the comparative decision table more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] comparative decision table is ready and can be shared.
- [ ] at least 4 common evaluation criteria measurement points are recorded.
- You ran a separate preventive step for the risk of mixing [ ] aspects.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** at least 4 common evaluation criteria
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **OpenAI Documentation - Guidelines**. OpenAI. 2026. Read: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides
2. **NIST AI Risk Management Framework**. NIST. 2023. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework

## Further reading

1. **OpenAI - Request Writing Guide** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering
2. **Nielsen Norman Group - Surface Copywriting** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/

# Lesson 11: Creating your own request directory

**In one sentence:** A good request is not a one-time idea, but a reusable tool.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** tagged request library v1

## Learning goal

Weekly customer communication tasks. In this situation, you are a sales support specialist and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
By the end of today's practical task, you will have a labeled request library v1 in your hand, which you can use again tomorrow.
Key insight: the request library starts working when you make the output measurable at startup. At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **build quickly retrievable requests for recurring tasks**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **tagged request library v1**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **10 tagged requests saved and documented**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step for managing the main risk: **always start from zero**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** tagged request library v1
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-rutin/nap-11-sajat-keres-konyvtar-letrehozasa.md`

## Who

**Primary Role:** Sales Support Specialist
**Secondary Role:** Sales Representative
**Involved decision maker:** sales manager

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows in practice how to apply the **request library** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- The library increases speed and quality at the same time if you maintain it.
- Quality requires two things: a good task description and consistent control.
- Quality comes from ensuring that tagged request library v1 is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- weekly recurring customer communication tasks in situations where a quick and clear decision is required
- when tagged request library v1 gives clear next step to multiple people

### Do not use this when

- if you cannot always cover the risk of starting from scratch with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between sales support specialist and sales representative
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where 10 tagged requests saved and documented count

## When

### Use it when

- you encounter weekly customer communication tasks and you have little time
- based on tagged request library v1, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- tagged request library v1 needs to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content does not always handle the problem of starting from scratch

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- tagged request library v1 is created faster, with fewer rounds
- quality will be more measurable 10 tagged requests saved and documented with indicators
- the rework is reduced, because you always filter out the error of starting from scratch early

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases in the case of weekly customer communication tasks
- harder to keep 10 tagged requests saved and documented goal

### Realistic expectation
- It will improve: the practical application of the request library and the quality of the transferable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying particular attention to **always starting from scratch** risk. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the v1 format of the tagged request library is clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether 10 tagged requests have been saved and documented as measuring points.


**Don't**
- Don't always leave the topic of starting from scratch in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Don't ask for a general text if you need material for a specific decision for weekly recurring customer communication tasks.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** an overly general request is initiated for weekly recurring customer communication tasks. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** no connection between output and 10 tagged requests saved and documented measurement. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] **tagged request directory v1** output is ready and saved.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the indicator **10 tagged requests saved and documented**.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a brief description of a real task from the following situation: **Weekly recurring customer communication tasks**
- a short own sample that shows what you consider a good request library output
- checklist to exclude the risk of always starting from scratch

### Steps

1. Write in 4 lines the situation, the goal and how you will measure 10 tagged requests, saving and documenting results.
2. Create a sample request to generate tagged request library v1, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Weekly customer communication tasks | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | tagged request directory v1 | Saved, reusable template |

> **Pro tip:** tagged request library v1 will be stable if you check it with the same 3 questions after every run.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose your own task for tomorrow, which requires the same type of decision as in the case of weekly customer communication tasks. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: 10 tagged requests saved and documented.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the tagged request library v1 more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] tagged request library v1 is done and ready to share.
- [ ] 10 tagged requests saved and documented, measurement points are recorded.
- [ ] you always ran a separate preventive step for risk starting from zero.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** 10 tagged requests saved and documented
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **Nielsen Norman Group - UX copywriting**. NN/g. 2024. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/
2. **Atlassian Teamwork Playbook**. Atlassian. 2025. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook

## Further reading

1. **NIST - AI Risk Management Framework** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework
2. **Atlassian - Teamwork Playbook** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook

quizQuestions.question: As a market researcher, you get this at 8:30 a.m.: Preparation of a summary containing market data. The deadline is 45 minutes, and the goal is to prepare a source control protocol. What should be your first decision?
options: I record the exact format of the output (source verification protocol) and next to it the measurement point (5 out of 5 statements supported by sources), and then I build the request on this.
I want a long answer right away for the task of creating a summary containing market data, without specifying format or constraints.
I only specify tone and assume the model will infer the rest about fact checking.
I skip quality control, even if the risk of passing on incorrect information is known.

options: Prepare two versions in a maximum of 140 words, tailored to the target audience, and at the end of both, show how the measurement supported by 5 sources out of 5 statements is fulfilled.
Write something short about the topic of creating a summary containing market data, the format and the quality gate are not important now.
Give a creative answer in any style, and we will clarify the source control protocol details later.
Rephrase the task in a nicer way, but let's not deal with the risk of passing on incorrect information separately.

quizQuestions.question: You receive the following feedback from this actor (content manager) for the task "Creating a summary containing market data": the output is too general and you cannot work from it immediately. Which fix is ​​the strongest next step?
options: I will rerun the request with a specific format, target audience, decision deadline and the expected structure of the source control protocol.
I will run the same request again, just in case the fact check results are better.
I remove constraints and optimize only for speed, even if the risk of passing on incorrect information increases.
I will pass it on without changes, and I will not record the measurement point (supported by 5 sources out of 5 statements) separately.

quizQuestions.question: Which measurement is the most suitable to prove the improvement of the work process, if the target indicator is: 5 out of 5 statements supported by sources?
options: In a real task, I measure whether 5 quality indicators supported by 5 sources out of 5 statements have actually been fulfilled.
I only check whether the output is longer than before, not whether the source control protocol is usable.

I only ask for an impression from the team, but I evaluate the fact-checking results without data.

quizQuestions.question: Which control habit best reduces this risk: passing on incorrect information?
options: Before sending, I run the 3-point quality gate: accuracy, management of transmission of incorrect information, next step.
I only look at language correctness, I do not check the actual usability of the source control protocol.
I consider the first answer of the model to be final, even if the goal supported by 5 sources out of 5 statements has not yet been met.
I use the same template for all tasks, ignoring the context of creating a summary with market data.

options: I start with a recurring task with a fixed 20 minute time slot and after each run I check 5 of 5 statements backed up by indicators.

# Lesson 14: Creating a personal "AI assistant" voice **In a sentence:** A personal AI assistant is good because it always brings the same quality.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** Role-based system instruction

## Learning goal

Handling several types of tasks in a uniform tone. In this situation, you are the office manager and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
By the end of today's practical task, you will have role-based system instructions.
Key insight: assistant system request starts working when you make the output measurable at start-up. At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **set up an AI assistant of constant quality**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **role-based system instruction**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **consistent output for 4 task types**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step to deal with the main risk: **inconsistent response quality**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** role-based system command
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-routine/day-14-personal-ai-assistant-voice-kialakitasa.md`

## Who

**Primary role:** office manager
**Secondary Role:** Team Assistant
**Involved decision-maker:** company manager

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows in practice how to use the **assistant system request** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- The system request provides a framework. This will make the result predictable.
- Fewer but more precise instructions typically give better results.
- Quality comes from ensuring that role-based system instructions is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- handling several types of tasks in a uniform tone in situations where a quick and clear decision is required
- when a role-based system instruction gives a clear next step to several people

### Do not use this when

- if you cannot cover the risk of inconsistent response quality with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between office manager and team assistant
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where a consistent output counts for 4 types of tasks

## When

### Use it when

- handling several types of tasks, you meet tasks in a uniform tone, and time is short
- based on role-based system instructions, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- role-based system instructions need to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content does not handle inconsistent response quality problem

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- a role-based system instruction is created faster, with fewer rounds
- quality will be more measurable with a consistent output indicator for 4 task types
- rework is reduced, because you filter out inconsistent response quality errors early

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases when handling several types of tasks in a uniform tone
- it is more difficult to maintain a consistent output target for 4 task types

### Realistic expectation
- It will improve: the practical application of assistant system requests and the quality of the transferable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying particular attention to **inconsistent response quality** risk. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the role-based system command format is clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether consistent output measurement points have been met for 4 task types.


**Don't**
- Don't leave the topic of inconsistent response quality in the background, even if the response seems good at first.
- Do not ask for a general text if a specific decision requires the handling of several task types in a uniform tone for a task.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** too general a request for the handling of several types of tasks in a uniform tone. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between output and consistent output measurement for 4 task types. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] You have completed and saved **role-based system instruction** output.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented the **consistent output for 4 task types** indicator.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a short description of a real task about the following situation: **Handling several types of tasks in a uniform tone**
- a short own sample that shows what you consider a good assistant system request output
- checklist to exclude the risk of inconsistent response quality

### Steps

1. Write in 4 lines the situation, the goal and how you will measure consistent output results for 4 task types.
2. Create a sample request to generate a role-based system statement, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | Handling several task types in a uniform tone | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | role-based system command | Saved, reusable template |

> **Common mistake:** Many people only look at the risk of inconsistent answer quality at the end, even though it should already be indicated in the task description.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose your own task for tomorrow, which requires the same type of decision as in the case of handling several types of tasks in a uniform tone. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: consistent output for 4 task types.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the role-based system statement more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] role-based system instruction is ready and can be shared.
- [ ] A consistent output measurement point is fixed for 4 task types.
- [ ] you ran a separate preventive step for the risk of inconsistent response quality.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** Consistent output for 4 task types
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **NIST AI Risk Management Framework**. NIST. 2023. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework
2. **Nielsen Norman Group - UX copywriting**. NN/g. 2024. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/

## Further reading

1. **Nielsen Norman Group - Surface Copywriting** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/
2. **NIST - AI Risk Management Framework** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework

options: I set the three-report template format and metric first: each target group gets one usable report before finalizing the prompt.
I want a long answer right away for the task of creating a weekly report in an operational and management view, without specifying format or constraints.
I only define tone and trust the model to infer report and analysis template details without explicit requirements.
I skip QA, even if there is a known risk of insufficient detail.

quizQuestions.question: The goal is to prepare 3 report format templates, and the risk is insufficient detail. Which request launch gives the highest chance of a usable first version?
options: Prepare two versions in max 140 words, tailored to the target audience, and at the end of both, show how 3 different usable report measurements are met for 3 target groups.
Write something short about the topic of creating a weekly report from an operative and managerial perspective, the format and the quality gate are not important now.
Give a creative answer in any style, and we will specify the details of 3 report format templates later.
Rephrase the task in a nicer way, but let's not treat the risk of inadequate detail separately.

quizQuestions.question: You receive the following feedback from this actor (operations manager) for the task "Creating a weekly report in an operational and managerial view": the output is too general and it is not possible to work from it immediately. Which fix is ​​the strongest next step?
options: I will rerun the request with a specific format, target audience, decision deadline and the expected structure of 3 report format templates.
I will run the same request again, just in case the report and analysis templates result will be better.
I remove the limits and just go for speed, even if the chance of not being detailed enough increases.
I hand it over unchanged and do not record the measurement point (3 different usable reports for 3 target groups).

quizQuestions.question: Which measurement is the most suitable to prove the improvement of the work process, if the target indicator is this: 3 different, usable reports for 3 target groups?
options: In a real task, I measure whether 3 different usable report quality indicators for 3 target groups have actually been met.
I am checking whether the text has become longer than before, regardless of the usability of 3 report format templates.
I am counting the number of times the request has been executed, even if inadequate verbosity persists.
I only ask for an impression from the team, but I evaluate the results of reports and analysis templates without data.

quizQuestions.question: Which audit habit best reduces this risk: inadequate granularity?
options: Before sending, I run the 3-point quality gate: accuracy, handling inadequate detail, next step.
I only look at the correctness of the language, I do not check the actual usability of the 3 report format templates.
I consider the first answer of the model to be final, even if 3 different usable report goals for 3 target groups have not yet been met.
I use the same template for all tasks, creating a weekly report in an operational and management view, ignoring its context.

quizQuestions.question: In your team, there is a lot of rework on the subject of report and analysis templates. Which sequence has the lowest risk of rework?
options: Goal and output definition (3 report format templates) -> specific request -> quality gate -> repair and save.
Quick request to create a weekly report in operational and management view for a task -> immediate sending -> subsequent rewriting if there is a problem.
Generic long prompt -> random reruns -> choose by gut feel, for 3 target groups 3 different usable reports without measurement.
Copy template -> skip risk of inadequate detail -> complete manual rewrite.

quizQuestions.question: Starting tomorrow, you would incorporate into your daily routine: 3 report format templates. Which launch plan is the most realistic as a beginner?
options: I start with a recurring task, with a fixed time frame of 20 minutes, and after each run I check 3 different usable report indicators for 3 target groups.
I introduce it to all tasks at the same time, even creating a weekly report in an operational and management view situation, without measuring and taking notes.
I only use it if there is already a problem, and I don't document the risk of insufficient detail separately.
I just read about the topic for a week, but I don't try the preparation of 3 report format templates on a real task.

# Lesson 19: Typical pitfalls in the given role **In a sentence:** A trap list improves faster than a firefight after the fact.
**Time:** 20-30 minutes
**Tangible output:** error countermeasure matrix

## Learning goal

AI-supported work in a dense futures environment. In this situation, you are a recruiter and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.
By the end of today's practical task, you will have an error countermeasure matrix.
Key insight: the prevention of role-specific errors starts to work when you make the output measurable at the start. At the end of the lesson, you will be able to: **assign preventive routines for typical errors**

### Success criteria (observable)
- [ ] The specific daily output is ready: **error countermeasure matrix**.
- [ ] At least one measurable indicator is attached to the output: **5 specific preventive steps for 5 error types**.
- [ ] There is a separate control step to deal with the main risk: **repeated errors and delays**.

### Output you will create today
- **Name:** error countermeasure matrix
- **Format:** editable board and short decision note
- **Where saved:** `ai-routine/day-19-typical-traps-in-the-given-role.md`

## Who

**Primary Role:** Recruiter
**Secondary Role:** Head of Admissions
**Involved decision maker:** HR director

## What

### What is this?

This lesson shows you in practice how to apply the **prevention of role-specific errors** method in a real work situation.
The goal is not a theory, but an output that you can really use in your next working day.

### What isn't this?

This is not a generic AI demo, and it is not busywork.

### 2-minute theory

- Prevention is cheaper than follow-up work. Pattern recognition is key.
- Quality requires two things: a good task description and consistent control.
- Quality comes from ensuring that the error countermeasure matrix is verifiable and repeatable.

### Key concepts

- **Working framework:** a short guide that describes the three objectives, format and limits.
- **Quality gate:** quick check before accepting the output.

## Where

### It works fine here

- in ai-supported work type situations in a dense deadline environment, where a quick and clear decision is required
- when an error countermeasure matrix gives several people a clear next step

### Do not use this when

- if you cannot cover the risk of repeated errors and delays with human control

### Connection points

- daily handovers between recruiter and hiring manager
- short statuses and decision preparation notes
- repetitive tasks, where 5 specific preventive steps count for 5 error types

## When

### Use it when

- you encounter an AI-supported work task in a tight deadline environment, and time is short
- based on an error countermeasure matrix, several actors must act in the same way

### Frequency

At least once per workday, and every time for recurring tasks.

### Late warning signs

- error countermeasure matrix needs to be rewritten every time
- you often get back that the content does not handle the problem of repeated errors and delays

## Why it matters

### Practical benefits

- error countermeasure matrix is created faster, with fewer rounds
- the quality will be more measurable for 5 error types with 5 concrete preventive step indicators
- rework is reduced, because you filter out repeated errors and delays early on

### What happens if you skip it

- the number of misunderstandings increases in an AI-supported work situation in a tight deadline environment
- it is more difficult to maintain 5 specific preventive step goals for 5 error types

### Realistic expectations

- It will improve: the practical application of preventing role-specific errors and the quality of the deliverable output.
- Not guaranteed: infallible response without human control in every run.

## How

### Step by step

1. Name the work situation and the expected result exactly. 2. Write down the mandatory limits (time frame, scope, style, prohibitions). 3. Request the first AI version in a specific format. 4. Run a 3-point quality gate, paying particular attention to the risk of **repeated errors and delays**. 5. Fix the output, then save it as a usable template.

### Do / Don't

**Do**
- Enter a short, specific task description so that the error countermeasure matrix format is clear from the start.
- Before finalizing, check whether 5 concrete preventive step measurement points for 5 error types have been met.


**Don't**
- Do not leave the topic of repeated errors and delays in the background, even if the answer seems good at first.
- Don't ask for a general text if you need material for a specific decision for an AI-supported work task in a dense deadline environment.

### Common errors and their correction

- **Error:** too general a request for ai-supported work situation in a dense deadline environment. **Correction:** name the person concerned, the deadline and the decision target.
- **Error:** there is no connection between the output and 5 specific preventive step measurements for 5 error types. **Fix:** record 1 binary pass/fail result at the end of each run.

### It's ready if

- [ ] You have completed and saved **error countermeasure matrix** output.
- [ ] You checked the output according to at least 3 quality criteria.
- [ ] You have documented 5 concrete preventive steps for **5 error types**.

## Guided exercise

### Inputs

- a brief description of a real task from the following situation: **AI-supported work in a dense deadline environment**
- a short sample of your own that shows what you consider a good role-specific error prevention output
- checklist to exclude the risk of repeated errors and delays

### Steps

1. Write in 4 lines the situation, the goal and how you will measure the results of 5 specific preventive steps for 5 types of errors.
2. Create a sample request to generate an error countermeasure matrix, then request two different versions.
3. Compare the versions in the table and mark which one can go on for real use.

### Expected output format

| Element | What do you type | Expected result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Situation | AI-powered work in a dense futures environment | Everyone involved understands from one reading |
| Request frame | Target + context + format + constraint | Minimum of 2 usable versions |
| Quality gate | Accuracy + Risk + Next Step | Clear repair decision |
| Final output | error countermeasure matrix | Saved, reusable template |

> **Pro tip:** error countermeasure matrix will be stable if you check it with the same 3 questions after each run.

## Independent exercise

### Task

Choose one of your own tasks for tomorrow where the same type of decision is required as in the case of AI-supported work performed in a dense deadline environment. Apply today's framework so that the end result can be measured in the same way: 5 specific preventive steps for 5 error types.

### Output

A short note with three blocks: situation, prompt variants, and quality gate. At the end, write a sentence about what you changed to make the error countermeasure matrix more accurate.

## Self-check

- [ ] error countermeasure matrix completed and can be shared.
- [ ] 5 concrete preventive step measurement points are recorded for 5 error types.
- [ ] You ran a separate preventive step for the risk of repeated errors and delays.
- [ ] You have a short decision note about why you chose the given version.

### Starting metric

- **Result:** 5 specific preventive steps for 5 error types
- **Date:** 2026-02-11
- **Tool used:** ChatGPT or other LLM

## Bibliography

1. **Nielsen Norman Group - UX copywriting**. NN/g. 2024. Read: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/writing-for-ui/
2. **Atlassian Teamwork Playbook**. Atlassian. 2025. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook

## Further reading

1. **NIST - AI Risk Management Framework** Why: it gives practical examples with samples that can be adopted immediately. Read: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework
2. **Atlassian - Teamwork Playbook** Why: it helps to confirm the method in real work situations. Read: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook

# Lesson 20: Skill measurement and leveling

**Time:** 20-30 minutes

## Learning goal

Carrying out a mini-project from task description to finished material. In this situation, you are a small business owner and you have to make a quick but reliable decision.

**Don't**
